lg.l: In the midnight of the cool season,
which seemed to cool the hills
so that the cowherds with curved staffs, [shiver]
rising on the right/forcefully and encircling [the world],
despised the full clamour [of the rains],
spreading their herds with bulls and lines [of cows] to a different pasture,
and being unsettled by the loneliness of shifting fields;
while their chaplets with long petals of white Malabar glory-lilies
[and] while the heavy dew that grips the body afflicted [them];
with firebrands clasped in their hands,
thrashing their cheeks [with that warmth, nevertheless] shiver,

l.hi: [the cool season, blowing harsh gusts of wind]

lg.l: so that the animals forget to graze, the female monkeys shiver,
birds perched [in trees] fall, and the milk-cows
while the white flowers, spotted on the outside, of the low-creeping bindweed
bloom on every bush together with the gold-like sponge gourd,
as soft-winged flocks of green-legged herons,
together with red-striped waders, seize [carps] everywhere
from the wet white sand over which dark silt has spread,
as carps stand out against the silt, the swift floods having subsided,
as the foaming white clouds, which had risen, the rains having diminished,
practise spray[ing] from the wide, big sky,
as ears of grain that emerge from the paddy with ample leaves,
swollen by the full rains in the wide field with beautiful areas,
as green unripe fruits with clear liquid,
[growing] in large clusters that contain multitudes, opened from rich palmyra stalks
on the sapphire-like necks of areca-palms, with whole bases,
ripen until they take on pulp
as brilliant drops of the branches that turn cold, hang [from them]
in wide groves with mixed blossoms, upon summits grown dense,
in the wide, long streets, which lie like rivers,
in the ancient town of abundance, where storied houses are high,
with leaf chaplets and big, beautiful, strong, firm shoulders,
having drunk toddy swarmed by bees, their delight and drunkenness increasing,
[being those who] care nothing for the cool drops of spray;
with green stalks and seasonal buds
emit fragrance at the stage of their opening, —
[those Jasmines,] poured onto plates by innocent women
with arms [like] silver-creepers [i.e., lightning] and rounded joints/
with rounded joints that have white bangles,
and rain [filled] eyes, eminent in grace,
which [extend] to rest upon [their] flower earrings,
light moist wicks in iron lamps,
scatter paddy and flowers, worship with [their] hands,
as the red-legged males of the doves that dwell in houses,
no [longer] ranging the village-common [for food] and eating
along with [their] females [in whom they] experience delight,
bewildered as to night and day and grown desolate,
perch, shifting [about] on the prop[-held] roost
while the small, short servants in the [well-]guarded, spacious mansion
grind many mixtures [into paste] on fragrant [grinding] stones resembling horse-gram,
while [people] abandon the pure, lustrous grindstone brought by Northerners,
along with sandalwood from the southern land,
and while the women—who do not adorn [their] tresses with garlands—
in order to put a few blossoms in [their] abundant dark tresses,
having ignited a fire with thorny twigs [steeped in] cool and fragrant unguent,
burn white powders along with dark-cored eaglewood,
are folded and hang on curved pegs,
in a lofty place, risen [as though] to touch the sky,
while, not moving about along the latticed windows with fine openings
that bring the southern wind to the beds in summer,
with joined panels, along with [their] bolts,
while, because of the noisy drizzling of sprays [i.e., because it is drizzling with rain],
being those who do not drink cool water from water pots with compact mouths,
finds satisfaction in red fires in the incense-burners with split mouths/
finds satisfaction in red fires with broad bases and split mouths,
in order to connect [the lute strings] so that they receive songs,
stroke, with the heat of their rounded, emerging breasts,
whose pleasing tones are altered by the cold,
and set in order the melodies of [their] small, dark-bodied lutes,
and while separated lovers feel lonely,

l.hi: Description of Constructing the Lofty Palace Gate

lg.l: without deviating from [its] state marked by the two poles,
did not incline to one side,
having placed threads with precision [on the walls],
having taken [note of] the directions and looked at the gods [of those directions],
partitioning the house suited for well-renowned kings,
within the rampart that rises high, having encircled [the house] altogether,
fitting together the glorious doors in pairs,
fashioning their new beams [with carvings of] female elephants
and blue water-lilies that open their buds
from an excellent timber fit for use (lit. taking), named after the star [Uttaram] too,
[and] because a dexterous artisan fitted them into union,
fit for joining together, constructed with a latch,
doors of lofty state, constructed like a hill
for elephant bulls to pass and enter,
along with banners that rise, having conquered [enemies],
[those doors] tall [in their] state, anointed with ghee,
to which white mustard has been applied without fault,
a front entrance where pure-coloured ganders
leap together with their short-legged geese,
in the frontyard of the lustrous mansion,
a long, white frontyard that receives the moon’s benefits,
together with the voice that expresses loneliness,
when the horse with its abundant mane,
which detests standing in the stable,
[and with all these features, stood the]
palace which resounds like the mountain slopes, dense with
such that [their] luxuriant long peacock feathers [near to the water-faucet], move;
[with] the singing of the waterfalls that becomes turbid and descends, becoming dense,
such that the water-spout with the split mouth of a Makara-gargoyle becomes full;

l.hi: Queen’s Chamber [Syntax: “Within the boundary (107) of this palace (100)]

lg.l: pouring ghee so that the wonderful lamp-bowls are full
which the figurines, glorious in their craftsmanship, fashioned by the Yavaṉar,
that are kindled from large wicks
and which stretch up their radiant heads
so that the spreading darkness departs
from each of the many different beds,
within the boundary of difficult protection,
to which men do not draw near,
except for the paragon of great excellence,
in the good house, pleasant to behold, named after the womb,
a single creeper with many beautiful flowers encircling
the constructed lofty walls, made as though of copper,
many sides, with banners laid like [rain]bows
[themselves] having the appearance as though one were seeing mountains,
[having the appearance] as though one were seeing sapphires,
with silver-like shining plaster applied [to them],
the renowned circular bedstead, which attained great size,
[elephants] with eminent and graceful lined foreheads,
so that [their] weight and refinement were equal,
interspersing fine leaves fashioned with a sharp chisel,
fitting them to garlic[-like] enduring bases,
the binding [of the bedstead] being glorious
so that fine jewels, glorious in their fittings, shine,
fixing broad bands, lustrous with the flowers
that had taken on the stripes of tigers
so that the [intermittent] spaces between the metal plates are concealed,
inscribing [upon the plates] a lion [/tiger] hunt,
having combined many dyed furs, being free from dust,
a mattress on which pure sheet expanded, fitted with flowers,
made of Kaliṅkam-cloth, washed and taken starch,
so that the many jasmine buds
from the forest of wide area are mixed up
placing broad pillows excellently upon the bed,
of a goose united with its companion,
so that the delicately spread mattress was excellent,
fit to be bound to her blossoming breasts
that dried up on her good forehead,
on her forearms overflowing with hair,
along with her ring of red lustre,
which [her] red fingers were made to wear
bent to resemble the split mouth
enjoined by a fine floral cloth,
into which pretty stains had crept,

l.hi: [as the queen] resembled an undecorated picture;

lg.l: pretty, bamboo[-like], plump and soft shoulders,
to which girdles were bound and tied,

l.hi: Foster-Mothers try and comfort the Queen

lg.l: as the foster-mothers with excellent faces,
their fragrant soft tresses mingled with fragrance,
having gathered as [her distress] became great,
repeating many words, both short and long,
she, who would not be persuaded,

l.hi: Queen remains inconsolable, so her attendant prays

lg.l: “for [the sake of that] lady, lustrous with goodness,
who, thinking about and looking at Rohiṇī,
who permanently dwells with her lord
with the orb of swelling speed,
as the sturdy-horned Goat (Meṣarāśi) comes first,
above the canopy made of newly made wax,
having attached rounded legs of firm condition,
to the plinths [of the bedstead] that were made to take [the shape]
joining her red fingers to the corners of [her] eyes,
held up by her large eyelashes,
so that her grievous and difficult affliction may end,

l.hi: King in the Battlecamp [Postposition Object – pācaṟai toḻil (188)]

lg.l: in order to see the excellent wounds from bright swords
who felled upon the battlefields bull-elephants
such that the long, round trunks
of [those] elephants, trained in their tasks,
as the great flames of the circular lamps,
whose heads rise and lean to the south,
swaying whenever the cool northern winds attack,
while the one who [goes] in front [the commander-in-chief]
shows [the king those soldiers’ wounds] one after another,
along with an enduring hard blade
to the top of which neem [leaves] are tied;
as proud horses of leaping motion
from which saddles are not removed,
with reins that have large ends
upon whose coverings bells are placed
scatter onto the dark-mudded streets the drops that dash [upon them],
having embraced on his left side
a beautiful cloth that falls along his flank,
he [being] one whose hand is placed
upon the neck of a strong young man
upon whose shoulders a sword had been strung,
as the spattering [rain] drops, moving intensely,
tied with threads to its base,
he who does not sleep even in the dense midnight,–
the king who wanders with a few,
his battle-camp task that opposed many?
